title: IRS Code 760 Meaning (Substantiated Credit Payment Allowance) – For Tax Professionals
description: IRS Code 760 indicates a substantiated credit payment allowance was posted. Learn what it usually means, how it affects balances, and what to check next for reversals, offsets, or notices.
content:
IRS Transcript Codes • Built for 750,000+ U.S. Tax Professionals

IRS Code 760 Meaning

IRS Code 760 typically means a substantiated credit payment allowance was posted to the tax module. In practice, it’s a credit entry that can reduce a balance due or contribute to an overpayment, depending on what else is on the transcript. This page shows what to verify, what sequences matter, and how to explain it to a client without guessing.

Try the parser (requires credits)

What is IRS Code 760?

Plain-English definition: IRS Code 760 means a substantiated credit payment allowance was posted to the account. It’s a credit entry recorded by the IRS for that module. ([irs.gov](https://www.irs.gov/irm/part20/irm_20-001-004r?utm_source=chatgpt.com))

Common transcript type
Account Transcript
Usually appears near
Codes 670, 706, 762
Typical context
Credit posting, balance updates, offset/reversal activity
Client-friendly summary
"The IRS posted an allowed credit"

When does Code 760 appear?

  • When an allowed credit is posted to the module: Code 760 is a credit allowance entry. ([irs.gov](https://www.irs.gov/irm/part20/irm_20-001-004r?utm_source=chatgpt.com))

  • During account/credit transfer workflows: 760 shows up in IRS internal payment/credit processing contexts.
  • When you’re reconciling balances: It’s most useful as a marker to confirm a credit is on-record for that specific tax period.

What does Code 760 usually signal?

If this appears and the balance due drops

The credit likely applied to tax, penalties, or interest on that module. Confirm the net balance lines and any follow-on interest activity.

If this appears but the balance due doesn’t change much

The credit may have been offset, applied elsewhere, or later adjusted. Don’t “explain” it until you validate the next entries.

If Code 762 follows

Treat that as a correction/reversal sequence and focus on the net effect across 760 and 762, not the headline code.

What should a tax professional do next?

  1. Confirm period + amount: Make sure the credit is on the correct year/period and matches the expected numbers.
  2. Validate net balance impact: Determine whether the credit reduced a balance due, created an overpayment, or was offset/reversed.
  3. Document the sequence: In the client summary, state what posted and what it changed. Avoid guessing “refund” unless the transcript supports it.

Example: how this looks in a parsed report

Below is a simplified view of how Code 760 can appear in a client-ready transcript report with context:

Credit posting timeline
Code 760 – Substantiated credit payment allowance
Amount: $1,500 • Date: 05/10/2024
Code 706 – Overpayment offset into a balance due module
Amount: $1,500 • Date: 05/12/2024
Code 971 – Notice issued / misc entry
Date: 05/15/2024

FAQ

What does IRS Code 760 mean on a tax transcript?

IRS Code 760 generally indicates a substantiated credit payment allowance was posted to the tax module. ([irs.gov](https://www.irs.gov/irm/part20/irm_20-001-004r?utm_source=chatgpt.com))

Does Code 760 mean my client is getting a refund?

Not necessarily. Code 760 is a credit posting. The outcome depends on the module balance and other activity (offsets, reversals, notices, or later adjustments).

What does it mean if Code 762 appears after Code 760?

It commonly indicates a correction/reversal related to a prior Code 760 entry. Focus on the net effect after both entries post.

What should I check next after Code 760 posts?

Confirm the amount/date and the tax period, then review offsets, reversals, and notices to determine what actually happened to the balance.
